stem: Let \( X = \{1, 2, \ldots, [[n]]\} \) carry the particular point topology with respect to \( p = [[p]] \): the open sets are exactly \( \varnothing \) together with every subset of \( X \) that contains \( p \) (one can check this collection is closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections). How many open sets does this topology have?
solution: A subset containing \(p\) is determined by freely choosing among the other \([[n]]-1\) elements, giving \(2^{[[n]]-1}\) such subsets; adding the empty set (which does not contain \(p\) but is open by definition) gives \(2^{[[n]]-1}+1 = [[answer]]\) open sets.
distractor_rationales: This is the open-set count of the DISCRETE topology, forgetting that only subsets containing \(p\) (plus \(\varnothing\)) are open here.
This forgets to add the extra open set \(\varnothing\), which does not itself contain \(p\).
This subtracts one instead of restricting to subsets containing \(p\).
This is a linear count, unrelated to the exponential count of subsets containing a fixed point.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: Let \( X = \{1, 2, \ldots, [[n]]\} \) carry the excluded point topology with respect to \( p = [[p]] \): the open sets are exactly \( X \) itself together with every subset of \( X \) that does NOT contain \( p \) (one can check this collection is closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections). How many open sets does this topology have?
solution: A subset not containing \(p\) is a subset of the other \([[n]]-1\) elements, giving \(2^{[[n]]-1}\) such subsets (this already includes \(\varnothing\)); adding \(X\) itself (which contains \(p\) but is open by definition) gives \(2^{[[n]]-1}+1 = [[answer]]\) open sets.
distractor_rationales: This is the open-set count of the DISCRETE topology, forgetting that only subsets avoiding \(p\) (plus \(X\)) are open here.
This forgets to add the extra open set \(X\), which itself contains \(p\).
This subtracts one instead of restricting to subsets avoiding \(p\).
This is a linear count, unrelated to the exponential count of subsets avoiding a fixed point.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.

stem: Let \( X = \{1, 2, \ldots, [[n]]\} \) and fix a subset \( S \subseteq X \) with \( |S| = [[k]] \). Define \( \tau = \{\varnothing\} \cup \{A \subseteq X : S \subseteq A\} \) (every nonempty open set must contain all of \(S\); this collection is closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections, so \(\tau\) is a valid topology on \(X\)). How many open sets does \(\tau\) have?
solution: A set \(A \supseteq S\) is determined by freely choosing among the remaining \([[n]]-[[k]]\) elements outside \(S\), giving \(2^{[[n]]-[[k]]}\) such sets; adding \(\varnothing\) (which does not contain \(S\) but is open by definition) gives \(2^{[[n]]-[[k]]}+1 = [[answer]]\) open sets.
distractor_rationales: This is the open-set count of the DISCRETE topology, forgetting the requirement that nonempty open sets must contain all of \(S\).
This forgets to add the extra open set \(\varnothing\).
This uses the single-point particular-point-topology formula (\(|S|=1\)) instead of the given \(|S|=[[k]]\).
This is a linear count, unrelated to the exponential count of subsets containing a fixed \(k\)-element set.
This is one more than the correct count.
This is one less than the correct count.
